l1: Question 1 — When did the VM boot issue first occur?
Question 2 — Is the problem affecting a single VM or multiple VMs?
Question 3 — Has the VM configuration been modified recently?
Question 4 — Is the VM stored on local disk or clustered storage?
Question 5 — Did the issue start after an OS patch or Hyper-V update?
l2: Advanced question 1 — Are there any changes in VM generation (Gen1 vs Gen2)?
Advanced question 2 — Is Secure Boot enabled and compatible with the guest OS?
Advanced question 3 — Are checkpoints or snapshots impacting VM state?
Advanced question 4 — Is the VM part of a failover cluster or replication setup?
Advanced question 5 — Are there performance or I/O errors reported before boot failure?

l1: Command or question 1 — Open Hyper-V Manager and check VM state
Command or question 2 — Run PowerShell Get-VM to confirm VM status
Command or question 3 — Review Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → Hyper-V-VMMS → Admin
Command or question 4 — Verify VM configuration file (.vmcx) integrity
Command or question 5 — Check if the VM’s VHDX file is accessible
l2: Advanced command 1 — Run Get-VMFirmware to check Secure Boot and boot order
Advanced command 2 — Use Get-VHD to validate VHDX health and state
Advanced command 3 — Run Get-ClusterResource if VM is clustered
Advanced command 4 — Review Hyper-V Replica logs if replication is enabled
Advanced command 5 — Run Test-VMReplicationHealth for replicated VMs

l1: Resolution step 1 — Restart the Hyper-V Virtual Machine Management Service
Resolution step 2 — Verify VM boot order and ensure OS disk is first
Resolution step 3 — Check VHDX file path and permissions
Resolution step 4 — Remove and re-add VM checkpoints if corrupted
Resolution step 5 — Ensure sufficient free disk space for VM startup
l2: Advanced fix 1 — Disable Secure Boot if guest OS is incompatible
Advanced fix 2 — Repair or recreate corrupted VHDX using Hyper-V Manager or PowerShell
Advanced fix 3 — Validate cluster configuration and re-register VM resource
Advanced fix 4 — Restore VM from backup or checkpoint if corruption persists
Advanced fix 5 — Escalate to Microsoft support if VM configuration files are unrecoverable

l1: Collection 1 — Export Event Viewer logs from Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → Hyper-V-VMMS → Admin
Collection 2 — Run Get-VM and export results
Collection 3 — Execute Get-VHD and save output
Collection 4 — Capture screenshot of Hyper-V Manager showing VM state
Collection 5 — Collect OS build and Hyper-V role version
l2: Advanced collection 1 — Export Get-VMFirmware configuration details
Advanced collection 2 — Collect cluster logs if VM is part of failover cluster
Advanced collection 3 — Gather performance monitor counters for Hyper-V VM startup
Advanced collection 4 — Export Hyper-V Replica health reports
Advanced collection 5 — Collect VM configuration files (.vmcx, .vmrs) for analysis

steps.action: Run Get-VM to confirm VM state
steps.expect: VM shows as Off or Boot Failed
Errors related to VM startup or configuration

steps.action: Run Get-VHD to validate disk health
Run Get-VMFirmware to check boot order
steps.expect: OS disk is first in boot sequence

steps.action: Restart VMMS service and adjust boot order
Run Get-VM and monitor Event Viewer
steps.expect: VM remains stable and boots without errors
